main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Physics-Informed Autoencoder (PIAE) for stochastic differential equations (SDEs)","Integration of empirical day/night NEE physics models with a Wiener process SDE","Latent space encoding of sensor measurements with deterministic and probabilistic decoders for SDE parameters and noise","Loss function combining Mean Squared Error (MSE) and Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD)"],"problems":["Missing values (gaps) in carbon dioxide (CO2) emission measurements from agricultural fields, especially at night","Improving the reliability and robustness of gap-filling and forecasting Net Ecosystem Exchange (NEE) from eddy covariance (EC) flux tower data","Enhancing data quality for CO2 emissions forecasting to support net zero goals"],"datasets":["Flux tower dataset (eddy covariance data) as described in Cumming et al. (2020)"],"metrics":["Mean Squared Error (MSE)","Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD)","Wasserstein distance","Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence","R2 value"],"results":[{"metric":"R2 value (compared to MDS)","value":"improved by 30% (as per RFR baseline, PIAE outperforms RFR)"},{"metric":"Uncertainty (compared to MDS)","value":"reduced by 70% (as per RFR baseline, PIAE outperforms RFR)"},{"metric":"MMD, Wasserstein, KL divergence","value":"PIAE shows significant improvement over state-of-the-art (exact values not specified)"}],"novelty_claims":["First use of a physics-informed autoencoder (PIAE) integrating stochastic differential equations (SDEs) for NEE gap-filling and forecasting.","Combines empirical day/night NEE models with a noise model in a unified SDE framework for robust and interpretable gap-filling.","PIAE accurately learns NEE distributions and SDE parameters, improving interpretability and robustness over state-of-the-art methods.","Demonstrates improved performance on both distribution-based and data-fitting metrics, especially for challenging nighttime NEE measurements and long data gaps."]}

methods: Physics-Informed Autoencoder (PIAE) for stochastic differential equations (SDEs)
Integration of empirical day/night NEE physics models with a Wiener process SDE
Latent space encoding of sensor measurements with deterministic and probabilistic decoders for SDE parameters and noise
Loss function combining Mean Squared Error (MSE) and Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD)
problems: Missing values (gaps) in carbon dioxide (CO2) emission measurements from agricultural fields, especially at night
Improving the reliability and robustness of gap-filling and forecasting Net Ecosystem Exchange (NEE) from eddy covariance (EC) flux tower data
Enhancing data quality for CO2 emissions forecasting to support net zero goals
datasets: Flux tower dataset (eddy covariance data) as described in Cumming et al. (2020)
metrics: Mean Squared Error (MSE)
Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD)
Wasserstein distance
Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence
R2 value

results.value: improved by 30% (as per RFR baseline, PIAE outperforms RFR)
reduced by 70% (as per RFR baseline, PIAE outperforms RFR)
PIAE shows significant improvement over state-of-the-art (exact values not specified)

novelty_claims: First use of a physics-informed autoencoder (PIAE) integrating stochastic differential equations (SDEs) for NEE gap-filling and forecasting.
Combines empirical day/night NEE models with a noise model in a unified SDE framework for robust and interpretable gap-filling.
PIAE accurately learns NEE distributions and SDE parameters, improving interpretability and robustness over state-of-the-art methods.
Demonstrates improved performance on both distribution-based and data-fitting metrics, especially for challenging nighttime NEE measurements and long data gaps.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Random Forest (RF) machine learning model","Integration of ground-based flux tower observations with remotely sensed data (fPAR, LAI) and ground-based variables (soil moisture, downward solar radiation, precipitation, mean air temperature)"],"problems":["Improving estimates of net ecosystem exchange (NEE) in semi-arid landscapes","Combining ground-based and remote sensing data for spatiotemporal NEE estimation","Identifying key predictors of NEE in complex landscapes"],"datasets":["Flux tower observations from Reynolds Creek Experimental Watershed (RCEW), southwest Idaho, USA","Remotely sensed data: fraction of photosynthetically active radiation (fPAR), Leaf Area Index (LAI)","Ground-based data: soil moisture, downward solar radiation, precipitation, mean air temperature"],"metrics":["Coefficient of determination (r2)"],"results":[{"metric":"r2 (coefficient of determination)","value":"0.87"}],"novelty_claims":["Demonstration of the use of machine learning (Random Forest) to combine multiple observational datasets (ground-based and remote sensing) for spatiotemporally extensive NEE estimation in a semi-arid landscape","Identification of the most important predictors (LAI, downward solar radiation, soil moisture) for NEE in the studied landscape","Validation of the RF model's temporal predictions at independent sites and years not used in model development"]}

methods: Random Forest (RF) machine learning model
Integration of ground-based flux tower observations with remotely sensed data (fPAR, LAI) and ground-based variables (soil moisture, downward solar radiation, precipitation, mean air temperature)
problems: Improving estimates of net ecosystem exchange (NEE) in semi-arid landscapes
Combining ground-based and remote sensing data for spatiotemporal NEE estimation
Identifying key predictors of NEE in complex landscapes
datasets: Flux tower observations from Reynolds Creek Experimental Watershed (RCEW), southwest Idaho, USA
Remotely sensed data: fraction of photosynthetically active radiation (fPAR), Leaf Area Index (LAI)
Ground-based data: soil moisture, downward solar radiation, precipitation, mean air temperature
metrics: Coefficient of determination (r2)
novelty_claims: Demonstration of the use of machine learning (Random Forest) to combine multiple observational datasets (ground-based and remote sensing) for spatiotemporally extensive NEE estimation in a semi-arid landscape
Identification of the most important predictors (LAI, downward solar radiation, soil moisture) for NEE in the studied landscape
Validation of the RF model's temporal predictions at independent sites and years not used in model development

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Continuous field measurements of net ecosystem exchange (NEE) of CO2 using automated soil CO2 flux system with transparent long-term chambers","Development and calibration of a model of net ecosystem exchange of CO2 incorporating environmental factors (air and soil temperature, PAR, water table depth)"],"problems":["Quantifying net ecosystem exchange (NEE), gross primary production (GPP), and ecosystem respiration (ER) in a ridge-hollow complex at Mukhrino Bog","Understanding the influence of environmental factors on NEE and the carbon budget of the bog"],"datasets":["Field measurements of CO2 fluxes at Mukhrino Bog (2017-2018) collected at large hollow and small ridge sites"],"metrics":["Net Ecosystem Exchange (NEE)","Gross Primary Production (GPP)","Ecosystem Respiration (ER)"],"results":[{"metric":"NEE (hollow site)","value":"–87.7 gC m^-2"},{"metric":"NEE (ridge site)","value":"–50.2 gC m^-2"},{"metric":"GPP (hollow site)","value":"–344.8 gC m^-2"},{"metric":"GPP (ridge site)","value":"–228.5 gC m^-2"},{"metric":"ER (hollow site)","value":"287.6 gC m^-2"},{"metric":"ER (ridge site)","value":"140.9 gC m^-2"}],"novelty_claims":["First continuous field measurements and modeling of NEE, GPP, and ER in a ridge-hollow oligotrophic bog in the Middle Taiga zone of West Siberia using automated chamber systems and environmental factor modeling.","Demonstration of the influence of environmental factors (temperature, PAR, water table depth) on carbon fluxes in this specific ecosystem."]}

methods: Continuous field measurements of net ecosystem exchange (NEE) of CO2 using automated soil CO2 flux system with transparent long-term chambers
Development and calibration of a model of net ecosystem exchange of CO2 incorporating environmental factors (air and soil temperature, PAR, water table depth)
problems: Quantifying net ecosystem exchange (NEE), gross primary production (GPP), and ecosystem respiration (ER) in a ridge-hollow complex at Mukhrino Bog
Understanding the influence of environmental factors on NEE and the carbon budget of the bog
datasets: Field measurements of CO2 fluxes at Mukhrino Bog (2017-2018) collected at large hollow and small ridge sites
metrics: Net Ecosystem Exchange (NEE)
Gross Primary Production (GPP)
Ecosystem Respiration (ER)
novelty_claims: First continuous field measurements and modeling of NEE, GPP, and ER in a ridge-hollow oligotrophic bog in the Middle Taiga zone of West Siberia using automated chamber systems and environmental factor modeling.
Demonstration of the influence of environmental factors (temperature, PAR, water table depth) on carbon fluxes in this specific ecosystem.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Decomposition of annual net ecosystem CO2 exchange (NEE) into phenological and physiological components (maximum carbon uptake (MCU), maximum carbon release (MCR), methods: Decomposition of annual net ecosystem CO2 exchange (NEE) into phenological and physiological components (maximum carbon uptake (MCU), maximum carbon release (MCR), carbon uptake period (CUP), and parameters α and β)
Analysis of long-term observed NEE from 66 eddy covariance sites
Use of global products derived from FLUXNET observations
Comparison with outputs from the Lund-Potsdam-Jena (LPJ) model and Multi-scale Synthesis and Terrestrial Model Inter-comparison Project (MsTMIP) models
problems: Identifying the processes driving the interannual variability (IAV) of net ecosystem CO2 exchange (NEE)
Determining which ecological indicators are most associated with the IAV of NEE
Assessing the accuracy of ecosystem models in capturing the drivers of IAV of NEE
datasets: Long-term observed NEE from 66 eddy covariance sites
Global products derived from FLUXNET observations
Multi-scale Synthesis and Terrestrial Model Inter-comparison Project (MsTMIP) model outputs
metrics: Explained variance (percentage of IAV of NEE explained by each component)
Model underestimation/overestimation of component contributions (percentage difference)

results.metric: Variance in IAV of NEE explained by MCU (global average)
Variance in IAV of NEE explained by α (global average)
Variance in IAV of NEE explained by CUP (global average)
Variance in IAV of NEE explained by β (global average)
Variance in IAV of NEE explained by MCR (global average)
Variance in IAV of NEE explained by CUP (water-limited ecosystems)
Variance in IAV of NEE explained by MCU (temperature- and radiation-limited ecosystems)
LPJ and MsTMIP models underestimation of MCU contribution
LPJ and MsTMIP models overestimation of CUP contribution

novelty_claims: First global-scale decomposition of NEE IAV into phenological and physiological components using long-term observations and global products
Identification that maximum carbon uptake (MCU) is the dominant driver of IAV of NEE at the global scale
Demonstration that current ecosystem models underestimate the role of MCU and overestimate the role of CUP in driving IAV of NEE
Provision of a new perspective on the proximate causes of IAV of NEE, emphasizing the importance of MCU variability for modeling global land surface carbon exchange

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Development of the REddyProc R package for post-processing eddy covariance (EC) flux data","u* threshold detection and filtering","Gap-filling of flux time series","Flux partitioning to estimate gross primary production (GPP) and ecosystem respiration (Reco)","Cross-year processing of flux data","Improved treatment of uncertainties, especially in u* threshold estimation and temperature sensitivity of respiration","Data import routines for various formats","Data visualization routines"],"problems":["Need for usable and extensible tools for post-processing of EC flux data after initial quality-checking and storage correction","Detection and filtering of low friction velocity (u*) periods to avoid bias in nighttime NEE","Filling gaps in (half-)hourly EC flux data due to instrument malfunction or non-representative conditions","Partitioning net ecosystem exchange (NEE) into GPP and Reco","Providing reproducible, automated, and easily integrated post-processing workflows for the EC community"],"datasets":["FLUXNET community datasets (implied by benchmarking against FLUXNET standard postprocessing)"],"metrics":["Monthly and annual fluxes (e.g., NEE, GPP, Reco)","Uncertainty estimates of u* threshold","Uncertainty estimates of gap-filled fluxes","Uncertainty in temperature sensitivity of respiration","Uncertainty in daytime partitioning"],"results":[{"metric":"Difference in monthly and annual fluxes (REddyProc vs. other tools)","value":"No significant differences across sites"},{"metric":"Uncertainty estimates of u* and gap-filled fluxes","value":"50% lower with REddyProc due to improved seasonal treatment in bootstrap analysis"},{"metric":"Uncertainty in daytime partitioning","value":"About twice as high with REddyProc due to better accounting for uncertainty in temperature sensitivity of respiration"}],"novelty_claims":["REddyProc provides a more usable and extensible R-based environment for standard CO2-focused post-processing routines than existing tools.","New features include cross-year processing and improved treatment of uncertainties, especially in u* threshold estimation and temperature sensitivity of respiration.","REddyProc achieves lower uncertainty in u* and gap-filled fluxes and more realistic uncertainty in daytime partitioning compared to state-of-the-art tools.","The package is easily installable, configurable, and integrates standard post-processing with extended analysis workflows."]}

methods: Development of the REddyProc R package for post-processing eddy covariance (EC) flux data
u* threshold detection and filtering
Gap-filling of flux time series
Flux partitioning to estimate gross primary production (GPP) and ecosystem respiration (Reco)
Cross-year processing of flux data
Improved treatment of uncertainties, especially in u* threshold estimation and temperature sensitivity of respiration
Data import routines for various formats
Data visualization routines
problems: Need for usable and extensible tools for post-processing of EC flux data after initial quality-checking and storage correction
Detection and filtering of low friction velocity (u*) periods to avoid bias in nighttime NEE
Filling gaps in (half-)hourly EC flux data due to instrument malfunction or non-representative conditions
Partitioning net ecosystem exchange (NEE) into GPP and Reco
Providing reproducible, automated, and easily integrated post-processing workflows for the EC community
datasets: FLUXNET community datasets (implied by benchmarking against FLUXNET standard postprocessing)
metrics: Monthly and annual fluxes (e.g., NEE, GPP, Reco)
Uncertainty estimates of u* threshold
Uncertainty estimates of gap-filled fluxes
Uncertainty in temperature sensitivity of respiration
Uncertainty in daytime partitioning

results.metric: Difference in monthly and annual fluxes (REddyProc vs. other tools)
Uncertainty estimates of u* and gap-filled fluxes
results.value: 50% lower with REddyProc due to improved seasonal treatment in bootstrap analysis
About twice as high with REddyProc due to better accounting for uncertainty in temperature sensitivity of respiration

novelty_claims: REddyProc provides a more usable and extensible R-based environment for standard CO2-focused post-processing routines than existing tools.
New features include cross-year processing and improved treatment of uncertainties, especially in u* threshold estimation and temperature sensitivity of respiration.
REddyProc achieves lower uncertainty in u* and gap-filled fluxes and more realistic uncertainty in daytime partitioning compared to state-of-the-art tools.
The package is easily installable, configurable, and integrates standard post-processing with extended analysis workflows.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Eddy covariance technique to measure CO2 fluxes","Analysis of seasonal and annual patterns of CO2 exchange","Statistical analysis of climatic factors (rainfall, radiation, temperature) affecting GPP, Reco, and NEE"],"problems":["Quantifying seasonal and annual patterns of net ecosystem CO2 exchange in a Brazilian seasonally dry tropical forest (Caatinga biome)","Determining whether the Caatinga acts as a carbon sink or source over multiple years","Identifying climatic factors controlling the variability of GPP, Reco, and NEE"],"datasets":["CO2 flux measurements from the Caatinga biome (2014 and 2015)","Climatic data (rainfall, global radiation, air and soil temperature)"],"metrics":["Net Ecosystem CO2 Exchange (NEE)","Gross Primary Production (GPP)","Ecosystem Respiration (Reco)","NEP/GPP ratio"],"results":[{"metric":"Annual cumulative NEE (2014)","value":"−169.0 g C m−2"},{"metric":"Annual cumulative NEE (2015)","value":"−145.0 g C m−2"},{"metric":"Annual NEP/GPP ratio (2014)","value":"0.41"},{"metric":"Annual NEP/GPP ratio (2015)","value":"0.43"}],"novelty_claims":["First quantification and evaluation of seasonal and annual CO2 exchange patterns in the Caatinga biome using eddy covariance.","Demonstrates that the Caatinga acts as a carbon sink even during the dry season and across multiple years.","Identifies key climatic drivers (global radiation, air and soil temperature) of diurnal carbon flux variability in a seasonally dry tropical forest."]}

methods: Eddy covariance technique to measure CO2 fluxes
Analysis of seasonal and annual patterns of CO2 exchange
Statistical analysis of climatic factors (rainfall, radiation, temperature) affecting GPP, Reco, and NEE
problems: Quantifying seasonal and annual patterns of net ecosystem CO2 exchange in a Brazilian seasonally dry tropical forest (Caatinga biome)

Identifying climatic factors controlling the variability of GPP, Reco, and NEE
datasets: CO2 flux measurements from the Caatinga biome (2014 and 2015)
Climatic data (rainfall, global radiation, air and soil temperature)
metrics: Net Ecosystem CO2 Exchange (NEE)
Gross Primary Production (GPP)
Ecosystem Respiration (Reco)
NEP/GPP ratio
novelty_claims: First quantification and evaluation of seasonal and annual CO2 exchange patterns in the Caatinga biome using eddy covariance.
Demonstrates that the Caatinga acts as a carbon sink even during the dry season and across multiple years.
Identifies key climatic drivers (global radiation, air and soil temperature) of diurnal carbon flux variability in a seasonally dry tropical forest.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Eddy-covariance flux measurements over 5 years (2014-2019) on a large, unirrigated green roof"],"problems":["Lack of knowledge about annual and seasonal variations of green roof carbon uptake due to meteorological conditions and water availability","Understanding the impact of precipitation and dry days on green roof carbon sequestration"],"datasets":["5 years (2014-2019) of eddy-covariance flux data from the BER green roof in Berlin, Germany"],"metrics":["Net ecosystem exchange (NEE) of carbon (g C m−2 y−1)","Annual precipitation","Number of dry days per year (DDY)","Measurement uncertainty"],"results":[{"metric":"Average annual net carbon uptake (NEE)","value":"−141.1 g C m−2 y−1"},{"metric":"Measurement uncertainty","value":"±15.5 g C m−2 y−1"},{"metric":"Maximum annual carbon uptake (NEE)","value":"−189 g C m−2 y−1 (in a wet year)"},{"metric":"Minimum annual carbon uptake (NEE)","value":"−95 g C m−2 y−1 (in a dry year)"}],"novelty_claims":["First multi-year (5-year) eddy-covariance study documenting consistent annual net carbon uptake on a large, unirrigated extensive green roof","Quantification of interannual variation in green roof carbon uptake as a function of precipitation and dry days","Demonstration that green roofs can act as consistent carbon sinks under current climate conditions, but may lose this function with increased dry periods in future climates"]}

results.value: −189 g C m−2 y−1 (in a wet year)
−95 g C m−2 y−1 (in a dry year)

novelty_claims: First multi-year (5-year) eddy-covariance study documenting consistent annual net carbon uptake on a large, unirrigated extensive green roof
Quantification of interannual variation in green roof carbon uptake as a function of precipitation and dry days
Demonstration that green roofs can act as consistent carbon sinks under current climate conditions, but may lose this function with increased dry periods in future climates

methods: Partitioning net CO2 flux measurements into fossil and biogenic components using tower-based CO and 14C measurements
Using a flux-gradient relationship to decompose CO2 fluxes
Estimating the CO to CO2 fossil fuel ratio with 14C isotope measurements to reduce uncertainty in flux decomposition
Applying a flux footprint model to quantify spatial variation in flux measurements and match them with inventory data
Comparing daily cycle and spatial structure of Hestia inventory emissions with eddy-covariance derived fossil fuel CO2 emissions on a seasonal basis

Uncertainty in high-resolution urban CO2 emissions inventories

Challenges in attributing urban CO2 flux measurements due to spatial heterogeneity and mixed sources/sinks

Hestia high-resolution urban CO2 emissions inventory
metrics: Mean difference in CO2 emissions (µmol m−2 s−1) between Hestia and eddy-covariance measurements (seasonal)
Percent difference in emissions (seasonal)
Correlation coefficient (r) of hourly mean fluxes (seasonal)
Relative magnitude of biological uptake and respiration compared to fossil fuel emissions

results.value: 0.36 µmol m−2 s−1 (3.2%) Hestia exceeds eddy-covariance
0.62 µmol m−2 s−1 (9.1%) Hestia exceeds eddy-covariance

results.metric: Average daytime biological uptake (cold season) as % of fossil fuel emissions
Average nighttime respiration (cold season) as % of fossil fuel emissions

novelty_claims: First direct comparison of source-partitioned eddy-covariance CO2 flux measurements with a high-resolution urban CO2 emissions inventory (Hestia) at high spatial and temporal resolution
Demonstrates the use of combined CO and 14C measurements to partition net CO2 fluxes in an urban environment and reduce uncertainty in source attribution
Provides evidence of significant biological CO2 exchange in northern cities during winter, challenging common assumptions
Applies a flux footprint model to directly match flux measurements with inventory data for spatially explicit evaluation

methods: Score-based generative modeling via stochastic differential equations (SDEs)
Reverse-time SDE for generative modeling
Neural network-based score estimation for time-dependent distributions
Numerical SDE solvers for sample generation

Unified framework encapsulating SMLD and DDPM as special cases
Controllable generation via conditional reverse-time SDEs for inverse problems (e.g., inpainting, colorization)
problems: Unifying and generalizing score-based generative models and diffusion probabilistic models
Efficient and flexible sample generation from complex data distributions
Exact likelihood computation for generative models

Improving sample quality and likelihood performance in generative modeling
metrics: Inception Score
Fréchet Inception Distance (FID)
Bits per dimension (bits/dim)
novelty_claims: Proposes a unified framework for score-based generative modeling using stochastic differential equations (SDEs) that generalizes and subsumes previous approaches (SMLD, DDPM).
Introduces the use of reverse-time SDEs for generative modeling, with neural network-based score estimation and numerical SDE solvers for sampling.
Develops Predictor-Corrector (PC) samplers that combine SDE solvers with score-based MCMC for improved sampling.
Derives a probability flow ODE equivalent to the SDE, enabling exact likelihood computation and efficient deterministic sampling.
Demonstrates controllable generation and solving of inverse problems (e.g., inpainting, colorization) using a single unconditional score-based model.

Achieves state-of-the-art results on CIFAR-10 for unconditional image generation (Inception Score 9.89, FID 2.20, bits/dim 2.99) and high-fidelity 1024x1024 image generation from a score-based model for the first time.

methods: Score-based generative model for graphs with a continuous-time framework
Graph diffusion process modeling joint distribution of nodes and edges via a system of stochastic differential equations (SDEs)
Novel score matching objectives tailored for the proposed diffusion process
New solver/integrator for the system of SDEs to efficiently sample from the reverse diffusion process
problems: Learning the underlying distribution of graph-structured data
Generating graphs that capture permutation-invariance and complex node-edge dependencies
Overcoming limitations of previous graph generative models (e.g., inability to model node-edge dependencies, inefficiency, lack of permutation invariance)
metrics: Generation quality (unspecified, but likely includes validity, uniqueness, novelty, and property-based metrics for molecules)

results.value: Outperforms existing one-shot generative models and achieves competitive performance to autoregressive models
Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines including autoregressive methods

novelty_claims: Proposes a novel score-based generative model for graphs using a continuous-time diffusion process via a system of SDEs that models both node features and adjacency matrices jointly
Derives new score matching objectives for the joint log-density in the proposed diffusion process
Introduces a new integrator/solver for the system of SDEs to enable efficient sampling
Demonstrates that the method can generate molecules close to the training distribution without violating chemical valency rules, effectively modeling node-edge relationships
